coredata.dc:title: Construction of interfacial energy transfer nanoplatforms using carbon dots and NaYF4:Eu3+ nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaoyi
Chen, Yeqing
Li, Rui
Zeng, Qingguang
Yu, Ting
coredata.dc:description: Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) is a well-known and important phenomenon in physics, chemistry, and biology, which plays a crucial role in various photofunctional systems, including photovoltaics, and lighting, and sensing where molecular distances and interactions are probed. This paper presents a systematical investigation of the construction of interfacial FRET between carbon dots (CDs) and NaYF4:Eu3+ nanoparticles (NPs) via various synthesis routes. The interfacial FRET was found to take place from CDs to Eu3+ in the wet-mixed and the reacted nanocomposites owing to the presence of strong interfacial interactions, as evidenced by the results of Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). By employing CDs-sensitized NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs as a model system, we were able to elucidate the intricate FRET mechanism between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. Surprisingly, the reacted nanocomposite exhibits remarkable photo- and thermal-stability, as well as high resistance to thermal degradation, demonstrating the robustness of the interfacial FRET in enhancing Eu3+ emission intensity. The study presents a convenient and versatile approach to achieving interfacial FRET based on CDs nanocomposites, which have a promising potential application in solid-state lighting and biomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Förster resonant energy transfer
Lanthanide ions
Nanocomposites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52002289), Science Foundation for High-Level Talents of Wuyi University (Grant No. 2019AL034) are gratefully acknowledged. This work is also supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2022A1515140032, 2022A1515011669), Major Science and Technology Research and Development Project of Jiangxi Province (20223AAE01003) and Science Foundation for Young Teachers of Wuyi University (No. 2019td03).

Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52002289) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2022A1515140032, 2022A1515011669) are gratefully acknowledged. This work is also supported by the Science Foundation for High-Level Talents of Wuyi University (Grant No. 2019AL034) Major Science and Technology Research and Development Project of Jiangxi Province (20223AAE01003) and Science Foundation for Young Teachers of Wuyi University (No. 2019td03).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CDs and (b) NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs (inset showing the corresponding HRTEM images); (c) XRD patterns of the dry-mixed, the wet-mixed, and the reacted nanocomposites with the standard PDF (JCPDS#77–2042) as a reference; (d) and (e)TEM images of the reacted nanocomposite at different magnifications (inset showing the corresponding HRTEM image).

A) FT-IR spectra of CDs powder, NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, the dry-mixed, the wet-mixed, and the reacted nanocomposites; b) XPS survey spectra of NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs and the reacted nanocomposite; XPS core-line spectra of (c) C 1s, and (d) O 1s in NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs and the reacted nanocomposite.

A) Normalized excitation and emission spectra for CDs powder (upper) and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs (lower); (b) emission and (c) excitation spectra for the dry-mixed, the wet-mixed, and the reacted nanocomposites, inset showing the digital photos of corresponding samples under daylight, UV light (λex = 340 nm).
Decay curves of (a) CDs emission at λ em = 450 nm (λ ex = 375 nm) and (b) Eu3+ emission at 613 nm (λ ex = 398 nm) in all samples, and the solid curves are the fitted curves.

Luminescence decay curves of (a) CDs emission at λ em = 450 nm (λ ex = 375 nm) in CDs powder and the reacted nanocomposites, and the solid curves are the fitted curves; (b) schematic diagram of the ET process from CDs to Eu3+.
Digital photos of CDs powder, NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, the dry-mixed, the wet-mixed, and the reacted nanocomposites under daylight and UV light with various exposure time.

(A) Temperature-dependent emission spectra for (a) CDs powder and (b) the reacted nanocomposite under excitation at 340 nm, inset of (a) showing the digital photos of CDs powder taken at 30 and 150 °C, under daylight and UV light, respectively; (c) the emission intensity ratio of 450 and 700 nm; (d) Four cycles of emission intensity variation for the reacted sample measured at 30–150 °C.

head.ce:title: Construction of interfacial energy transfer nanoplatforms using carbon dots and NaYF4:Eu3+ nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Applied Physics and Materials, Wuyi University, Jiangmen, 529020, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Applied Physics and Materials, Wuyi University, Jiangmen, 529020, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Applied Physics and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) is a well-known and important phenomenon in physics, chemistry, and biology, which plays a crucial role in various photofunctional systems, including photovoltaics, and lighting, and sensing where molecular distances and interactions are probed. This paper presents a systematical investigation of the construction of interfacial FRET between carbon dots (CDs) and NaYF4:Eu3+ nanoparticles (NPs) via various synthesis routes. The interfacial FRET was found to take place from CDs to Eu3+ in the wet-mixed and the reacted nanocomposites owing to the presence of strong interfacial interactions, as evidenced by the results of Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). By employing CDs-sensitized NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs as a model system, we were able to elucidate the intricate FRET mechanism between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. Surprisingly, the reacted nanocomposite exhibits remarkable photo- and thermal-stability, as well as high resistance to thermal degradation, demonstrating the robustness of the interfacial FRET in enhancing Eu3+ emission intensity. The study presents a convenient and versatile approach to achieving interfacial FRET based on CDs nanocomposites, which have a promising potential application in solid-state lighting and biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) is a highly effective energy transfer (ET) mechanism based on the electric dipole-electric dipole interaction, which has been extensively studied in various scientific fields, including physics, chemistry, and biology. The efficiency of a FRET system is contingent upon the spectral overlap of the coupled emitters, the relative orientation of the transition dipole moments, and the distance between the donor and acceptor [1,2]. In particular, the distance sensitivity mechanism has been widely used to construct responsive fluorescent probes, which can provide structural information or monitor dynamic processes in photofunctional systems, due to the strong R −6 distance dependence of the transfer probability (R being the donor-acceptor distance) [3–5]. Consequently, the construction of FRET nanoplatforms presents a multitude of intriguing avenues for the development of optical functionality at the nanometer scale.

Lanthanide (Ln3+) ions doped nanomaterials have attracted considerable research attention due to their distinctive optical properties, including sharp emissions and long luminescence lifetimes, as well as the ability to emit multiple wavelengths across a broad spectrum [6–10]. Benefitting from a fairly large number of 4f energy levels, Ln3+ ions are optimal for the construction of efficient ET systems for a multitude of applications [11]. The low optical absorption and the narrow bandwidths of Ln3+ donors, however, result in weak emission intensity and low efficiency, which seriously hinders the applications of Ln3+ ions doped nanomaterials [12]. To build efficient FRET-based nanoplatforms with Ln3+ ions doped nanocrystals as the acceptors, significant efforts have been made to seek the broadband sensitizers, including organic dyes, noble metal nanoclusters, and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), etc [13–15]. These studies have significantly advanced the development of FRET-based nanoplatforms with potential applications in the fields of bioimaging and optical communication.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials with size <10 nm, have aroused extensive attention in a wide array of research fields, owing to their exceptional properties such as tunable emission, low toxicity, facile preparation, and easy functionalization [16–21]. Most importantly, the two typical advantages of strong blue-green emission and abundant surface groups make CDs ideal candidates as light-harvesting materials to sensitize the Ln3+ emission by constructing nanocomposites [22,23]. Recently, there has been a great deal of research into the combination of CDs and Ln3+ ions with the aim of boosting the Ln3+ luminescence through FRET. Samanta et al. observed strong Eu3+ emissions due to FRET from CDs to Eu3+ upon exciting the CDs band in CDs/LaF3 nanocomposites [24]. Huang et al. fabricated CDs@MgWO4:Ln3+ nanostructures using a simple hydrothermal method [25]. For the CDs@MgWO4:Ln3+ nanosheets, a dramatic luminescent enhancement was observed under excitation of 263 nm, attributed to the FRET from CDs to Ln3+. Very recently, Wang et al. investigated the interfacial FRET from surface-active CDs to Eu3+ in new composites made by combining the porous CaMoO4:Eu3+ host and the embedded CDs [26]. Specifically, a variety of combination methods have been employed to fabricate the CDs-Ln3+ hybrid nanomaterials, which have been demonstrated remarkable properties that significantly expand their applicability in areas such as fluorescent probes, anti-counterfeiting, and bioimaging [12]. Despite previous efforts, the in-depth ET mechanism between CDs and Ln3+ still requires further quantitative investigation. In particular, the interfacial FRET in large-sized CDs-Ln3+ hybrid materials (>100 nm) with irregular morphology is challenging due to the critical distance required for FRET (approximately less than 10 nm) between energy donors and acceptors. On the other hand, the photo- and thermal-stability of nanocomposites is the one most often ignored issues, yet it plays a pivotal role in the efficacy and color stability of solid-state light devices.

In light of these considerations, we conducted a comprehensive investigation into the fabrication of interfacial FRET nanoplatforms based on CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ nanoparticles (NPs) through a range of synthesis methodologies. To achieve this, NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs with an average size of 20 nm serve as energy acceptors, while the most common CDs with strong blue emission serve as energy donors, prepared by citric acid and urea. The interfacial interactions between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. It has been demonstrated that there are robust interfacial interactions between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs in both the wet-mixed and the reacted nanocomposites. The interfacial FRET mechanisms were investigated by means of steady-state and dynamic-state fluorescence spectrometers. Notably, the reacted nanocomposite exhibits a superior photo- and thermal-stability, suggesting considerable potential for a diverse range of applications.

Materials: Branched polyethylenimine (PEI, 10 kDa) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. NaCl (99 %) were purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Citric acid (≥99.5 %), urea (≥99 %), ethylene glycol (EG, 99 %), YCl3·6H2O (99.99 %), EuCl3•6H2O (99.99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. NH4F (≥98 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ethanol (AR, 98 %) was obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagents. All materials were utilized without any further purification, and deionized water was used throughout this study.

Synthesis of CDs: The CDs were fabricated using the previously reported method with certain modifications. Citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were dissolved in 10 ml deionized water, stirred for 10 min until complete dissolution was achieved, and the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. After reacting under 160 °C for 6 h, the resulting solvent was further mixed with 20 mL ethanol and then centrifuged at 9000 r min−1 for 5 min. The precipitate was collected, dispersed in ethanol, and centrifuged (9000 r min−1, 5 min) twice to wash off residual solvents and small organic molecules, and then freeze-dried to gain dark product of CDs. Finally, the CDs powders were maintained at 4 °C for the next step.

Synthesis of 1.2 mmol NaYF
4
:Eu
3+
NPs: In a typical experiment, 4.8 mmol NH4F and 12 mL ethylene glycol were added into a beaker and both stirred for 30 min to form solution A. NaCl (2.4 mmol), YCl3·6H2O (1.08 mmol), EuCl3·6H2O (0.12 mmol), and PEI (0.3 g) were completely dissolved in 18 mL of ethylene glycol. After stirring for 30 min, the solution A was added into the mixture. Subsequently, the solution was slowly transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted for 8 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 5 min, then washed repeatedly with ethanol and centrifuged three times. Finally, the precipitate was obtained by drying in air at 60 °C overnight in an oven and ground fully to obtain NaYF4: Eu3+ powders.

Synthesis of CDs/NaYF
4
:Eu
3+
nanocomposites: For the “dry-mixed” nanocomposites, 50 mg CDs powders and 1.2 mmol NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs were thoroughly ground in a mortar to obtain dry-mixed samples. In the case of the “wet-mixed” nanocomposites, 50 mg CDs powders and 1.2 mmol NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs were mixed in a vial and dispersed in 20 mL of water. The mixing process was enhanced by sonication in an ultrasonic bath for 2 h. The precipitate was subsequently collected by centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 5 min without washed. Finally, the precipitate was obtained by drying in air at 60 °C overnight in an oven and ground fully to obtain wet-mixed samples. For the “reacted” nanocomposites, a similar synthesis process was employed, whereby 50 mg of the obtained CDs powders were added into the mixtures before sealing them in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. This process was conducted in accordance with the synthesis of 1.2 mmol NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs.

Characterization: The phase purity of solid-state samples was checked by a PanAnalytical X'Pert powder diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Microstructures and interplanar crystal spacing were observed by TEM (JEM-F200) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). XPS was taken on an ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Scientific) spectrometer equipped with X-ray source (hv = 1486.6 eV, monochromatic Al Ka, 150 W). FT-IR spectra were obtained by a Vertex PerkinElmer 580BIR spectrophotometer (Bruker) through mixing the sample with tableting of KBr. Steady state and dynamic spectra analysis were performed on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a single 0.22 m excitation and emission monochromator. For recording excitation and emission spectra, we used a 450 W Xe lamp as the excitation source and a Hamamatsu R928 PMT for detection of visible emission. For temperature dependent emission measurement, the samples were heated in a Linkam THMS600 temperature controlled stage. To remove the absorbed H2O, which has a deleterious effect on the emission intensity, the sample was first heated to 100 °C for 5 min, and then cooled to room temperature.

The as-prepared CDs powder and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs were initially characterized with TEM to determine their size and morphology. The TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows the as-prepared CDs powder to be highly dispersed, with the CDs exhibiting a torispherical morphology and an average size of 2.5 nm, which is consistent with previously reported results [27,28]. The HRTEM analysis shows a lattice fringe distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite (insets of Fig. 1a), thereby confirming the graphite structures of CDs. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of the as-prepared NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, which are uniform and monodisperse nanospheres with a mean diameter of 20 nm as previously reported in Ref. 29. The cubic NaYF4 structure is revealed in the HRTEM image shown in the inset of Fig. 1b, with an observed lattice distance of 0.191 nm, which is in good agreement with the d-spacing for the (220) lattice plane of the NPs. The results indicate the successful synthesis of CDs powder and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. The small size of the nanospheres is conducive to FRET. Through such a simple mixing method, the dry-mixed nanocomposite and the wet-mixed nanocomposite can be obtained. The phase components of both mixtures were confirmed by XRD, as shown in Fig. 1c. The XRD pattern shows well-indexed, sharp diffraction peaks indicative of cubic NaYF4 crystals with a = 5.47 Å (JCPDS card No. 77–2042) [30]. A slight band is observed at approximately 2θ = 22°, particularly for the dry-mixed nanocomposite, which can be attributed to amorphous CDs as reported in the literature [31,32]. The XRD pattern of the reacted nanocomposite, synthesized by the hydrothermal method, is similar to that of the mixed nanocomposites. This indicates that the hydrothermal method was successful preparing the reacted nanocomposite, and that the crystal form of NaYF4 in the nanocomposite was not altered by CDs. Fig. 1d displays the TEM image of the reacted nanocomposite, showing a uniform spherical morphology with a diameter of approximately 20 nm. This indicates that the morphology of the NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs remains unchanged. The attachment of CDs onto the surface of NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs is not readily apparent, potentially due to the low content of CDs in the system. This is in agreement with the relatively weak peak of CDs observed in the XRD pattern shown in Fig. 1c. The HRTEM image of the reacted nanocomposite, as shown in Fig. 1e, reveals the presence of a few torispherical CDs on the surface of NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. The inset of Fig. 1e shows the presence of two distinct sets of lattice fringes. The narrow interlayer spacing of 0.166 nm in the central region corresponds to the lattice spacing of the (311) lattice plane of cubic NaYF4, while the wider interlayer distance of 0.21 nm on the surface corresponds to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon. This result corroborates the existence of CDs in the reacted nanocomposite.

In order to ascertain the elemental composition and bonding configuration of the samples, FT-IR spectra and XPS were performed. FT-IR spectra of the CDs powder, NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, the dry-mixed, the wet-mixed, and the reacted nanocomposites are displayed in Fig. 2 a. In a comparative analysis, the absorption peaks of the dry-mixed nanocomposite are similar to those of the CDs powder, with the N–H stretching band (the maxima at ∼3400 cm−1), the O–H stretching band (at ∼3220 cm−1), and the C=C stretching band (at ∼1600 cm−1) being the most notable. Nevertheless, the presence of a sharp, middle-intensity peak at about 505 cm−1, corresponding to the Y–F stretching vibration, suggests the presence of NaYF4 NPs in the dry-mixed nanocomposite. As previously documented in the literature, the dry-mixed nanocomposite typically exhibits heterogeneous intermixing on the microscopic scale. Because of the greater distance on the scale of a few hundred nanometers, the interfacial interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs will be relatively weak. This is in accordance with previous findings in the literature [33–35]. The peaks at 1630 and 2975 cm−1 in the NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs are indicative of the characteristic vibration of amide bonds and the asymmetric stretching vibration of methylene units, respectively. This confirms the presence of PEI on the NPs, which is consistent with previously reported results in the literature [13,36]. Compared to the NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, the CH2 stretching vibration (2975 cm−1) was nearly absent in the FT-IR spectra for the wet-mixed and the reacted nanocomposites, suggesting an interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. This can be explained by the surface ligand exchange of PEI coated NPs with carboxylic ligands, which is enabled by the strong coordination ability of the COOH with Ln3+ ions [37,38]. This conclusion is supported by the abundance of carboxylic ligands on the surface of CDs, as evidenced by the distinct absorption band at 1675 cm−1 (see Fig. 2a). Unfortunately, the adsorption peaks of methylene units and carboxyl groups overlap almost completely, making it difficult to distinguish between them, particularly for the reacted nanocomposite. Nevertheless, the strong interfacial interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs was further verified by the XPS measurements. The full-scan XPS spectra for NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs and the reacted nanocomposite, including Na, Y, F, Eu, C, and O elements, are shown in Fig. 2b. In the case of the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 2c), the main peak can be divided into three typical peaks centered at 284.8 eV (C=C/C–C), 286.2 eV (C–O/C–N), and 288.3 eV (C=O). Similarly, the deconvoluted O 1s core-line spectra exhibit three different chemical structures (see Fig. 2d), representing the C=O (531.3 eV), C–O (532.8 eV), and metal oxide (536.6 eV). It was observed that the proportion of C=O species, which is primarily derived from carboxyl groups, exhibited a notable increase in the reacted nanocomposite. Meanwhile, the binding energy of the metal oxide in the reacted nanocomposite (∼536.8 eV) is observed to exhibit a clear shift towards a lower energy compared to that of NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs (∼536.6 eV). The evidence further demonstrates that CDs have been successfully decorated on the surface of NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs with robust interfacial interactions in the reacted nanocomposite, in agreement with the observed HRTEM image in Fig. 1e. It should be noted that the direct observation of the homogeneous intermixing of these nanocomposites by means of energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) is challenging due to the interference from coexistent elements in the two materials.

Based on the above structural analysis, it can be inferred that the interfacial interactions between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs are distinct for these three nanocomposites. The robust interfacial interactions between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs will facilitate the occurrence of interfacial FRET, because the distance between energy donors and acceptors should fall within the critical distance for fluorescence resonance ET [39,40]. Prior to obtaining evidence for interfacial FRET, we conducted a preliminary investigation into the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the CDs powder and the NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs at room temperature. Fig. 3 a presents the normalized excitation and emission spectra of the CDs powder and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. The excitation spectrum of the CDs powder shows a broad band centered at 340 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O bond of the CDs. Upon excitation at 340 nm, a blue emission band centered at 450 nm is observed (see Fig. 3a). This indicates that the intrinsic blue emission of the CDs powder persists, despite the weaker emission observed in the solid state compared to that of the CDs solution, which is due to serious self-quenching in the solid state. Upon excitation of NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs at 398 nm, several emission lines were observed in the red region. These lines were assigned to the typical 4f-4f transitions of Eu3+:5D0→7F J (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4). It should be noted that the emission intensity of the 5D0→7F1 forced electric-dipole transition (peak at 593 nm) is comparable to that of the 5D0-7F2 magnetic dipole transition (peak at 613 nm). This can be explained by the fact that the crystallographic site symmetry of Eu3+ descend from O
h to C
s (or C 2) in cubic NaYF4. This result is consistent with the previous reports on Eu3+-doped cubic NaYF4 NPs [41]. By monitoring the red emission (λ em = 613 nm), a multitude of sharp excitation peaks were observed, the most notable of which were between 360 and 410 nm and at 460 nm. These peaks can be assigned to the intraconfigurational transitions to higher energy 4f 6 levels. It can be found that the broadband emission of CDs powder overlaps with numerous Eu3+ excitation lines between 390 and 520 nm, especially for the 5D2 level of Eu3+ at 460 nm, which is a prerequisite for achieving efficient FRET. Note that the excitation spectrum depicted in Fig. 3a reveals a relatively large energy gap between the 5H3 and 5D4 levels, spanning a range of 335–365 nm. This indicates that Eu3+ ions cannot be sufficiently excited to give rise to emission at those excitation wavelengths. In the case of FRET, direct excitation of CDs in the excited state (i.e., λ ex = 340 nm) will result in red emission from the 5D0 level of Eu3+ and give rise to additional Eu3+ emission lines, which serve as a fingerprint for FRET.

To test this hypothesis, we measured the excitation and emission spectra for all nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 3b and c. Upon excitation at 340 nm, the emission spectra of the three nanocomposites exhibited distinct characteristics. The dry-mixed nanocomposite shows a typical broadband blue emission origin from CDs, indicating the absence of ET from CDs to Eu3+. As anticipated, the wet-mixed and the reacted nanocomposites display comparable emission behaviors, consisting of a broadband emission from CDs and several narrow emission peaks from Eu3+, suggesting ET from CDs to Eu3+. This can be explained by the robust interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, occurring at the nanometer scale for the wet-mixed and the reacted nanocomposites. Note that the emission intensity of the reacted nanocomposite is considerably higher than that of the wet-mixed nanocomposite, indicating a stronger interfacial interaction for the reacted nanocomposite. This result is consistent with the expected fact that high temperature and high pressure will facilitate the bonding interaction between the carbonyl group in CDs and the surfaced Y cation in NaYF4 NPs during the hydrothermal reaction [42]. The insets of Fig. 3b show photographic images of the luminescent color under daylight and ultraviolet (UV) light. The reacted nanocomposite produces a perfect bright white light, resulting from the mix of blue and red light, which is clearly visible to the naked eye. Additional evidence for the ET between CDs and Eu3+ is provided by measuring the excitation spectra of a characteristic Eu3+ emission line for these nanocomposites (see Fig. 3c). By monitoring the Eu3+ emission at 613 nm, the excitation spectrum of the dry-mixed nanocomposite shows several weak sharp peaks that are similar to those observed in NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. This indicates that Eu3+ sensitization by CDs does not occur in this system. In contrast, the excitation spectra of the reacted and the wet-mixed nanocomposites consist of numerous sharp lines superimposed on a broad band. With the exception of the narrow excitation lines of Eu3+, the broad excitation band is well overlapped with the characteristic absorption of CDs, thereby confirming that ET occurs from CDs to Eu3+.

The robust interfacial interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs will facilitate the interfacial ET in nanocomposite, as evidenced by the luminescence decay curves. The luminescence decay curve of the CDs powder (λem = 450 nm) presents a slight deviation from a single exponential, which can be fitted by a double exponential function with an average lifetime of 7.8 ns. This value is consistent with the reported lifetime of CDs in Ref. 43. The decay curves of CDs emission in the all nanocomposites become non-exponential and faster. In particular, the decrease in lifetime for the dry-mixed nanocomposite is likely attributable to the damage to the surfaces of CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs that occurs as a consequence of the physical mixing process. This is corroborated by the diminished Eu3+ emission and shortened lifetime, as illustrated in Fig. 3c and 4 b. This may be associated with the inferior stability, which will be discussed in greater detail later. Differently, the decay curve of CDs emission for the wet-mixed nanocomposite presents faster and more nonexponential decay characteristics, especially at the beginning of the curve (Fig. 4a). This indicates that there is the strong interfacial interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, which increases the probability of energy dissipation except for the ET to Eu3+. The shortened lifetime of Eu3+ emission is clear evidence of the strong interfacial interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, which causes severe mutual emission quenching (Fig. 4b). Interestingly, the luminescence decay curves of the reacted nanocomposite exhibit a resemblance to the intrinsic ones (see Fig. 4). The slight decrease in decay lifetime (∼9.3 m s) may be attributed to the surface variation resulting from the interfacial interaction in the reacted nanocomposite. This indicates that the tightly coupled system facilitates the transfer of energy from CDs to Eu3+, impeding the back ET, which gives rise to terrible mutual quenching.

To gain further insight into the interfacial ET processes between CDs and Eu3+ in nanocomposites, luminescence decay curves of related samples were recorded for CDs emission, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Due to the significant mutual quenching observed in the wet-mixed nanocomposite, it is challenging to quantify the ET rate and efficiency from CDs to Eu3+ ions. The study focuses on the ET processes of the reacted nanocomposite. As anticipated, the excited-state lifetime of CDs emission was observed to decrease with increasing Eu3+ concentration for the reacted nanocomposites (Fig. 4a). This indicates the presence of an additional decay channel for the CDs in the nanocomposites, which can be attributed to the ET from CDs to NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs through a non-radiative process. This further substantiates the occurrence of interfacial ET from CDs to Eu3+ in the reacted nanocomposites. It should be noted that radiative transfer from CDs to Eu3+ may be also occur, but would not result in a decrease in the lifetime of the energy donor. The average lifetime allows for the estimation of the non-radiative transfer efficiency, which is 7.2 % for the 5 % Eu3+ nanocomposite and 12.5 % for the 10 % Eu3+ nanocomposite, respectively. According to the Foster-Dexter theory for ET via dipole-dipole interaction, there is a critical distance (R
c ) between the energy donor and the acceptor, which is defined as the distance at which the radiative and ET rates are equal. The critical distance R
c can be given by the following expression [44].
(1)
R c 6
= 3 ×
10 12
f A
E
− 4
S O
where
f
A is the oscillator strength of the optical absorption transition on acceptor, E represents the energy of maximum spectral overlap (in eV), and SO stands for the spectral overlap integral. Due to the forbidden nature of the f-f transitions on Eu3+, the oscillator strength
f
A is relatively small, with a value of 10−6. Based on the spectral overlap from the broadband emission of CDs and the sharp excitation spectrum of Eu3+ (see Fig. 3a), an estimate of SO can be determined to be 0.2 eV. This allows for the estimation of a critical distance for FRET from CDs to NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs to be 5 nm. This indicates that FRET between CDs and Eu3+ typically works effectively only for the distances within 5 nm. For NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs, Eu3+ ions situated in proximity to the surface will be exhibit a markedly enhanced degree of sensitization in comparison to ions situated within the core of the nanocrystal. Consequently, the transfer efficiency can be enhanced by reducing the size of the NPs or increasing the concentration of Eu3+. However, the limitations of the hydrothermal method make it challenging to precisely control the size of NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. It would be beneficial to explore alternative methods for synthesizing Ln3+ ions doped ultrasmall NPs for the construction of efficient FRET nanoplatforms. Based on the preceding analysis, a simple schematic diagram of the ET mechanism between CDs and Eu3+ ions is proposed, as shown in Fig. 5b. Upon absorbing UV radiation, the electrons in the ground state are excited to the excited state of CDs. Besides the radiation transition from the excited state of CDs that accompanies the emission of blue photons, there is a nonradiative ET pathway from the excited state of CDs to the Eu3+: 5DJ (J = 0, 1, 2, and 3) levels, resulting in the typical Eu3+ emission from the 5D0→7F J (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4) transitions followed by fast multi-phonon relaxation to the 5D0 level.

In discussions of the optical properties of NPs, the topic of photostability is frequently raised. For example, CDs typically demonstrate robust photostability in aqueous solutions. However, they are prone to oxidation and degradation in the solid state when exposed to light, moisture, or heat. Here, we examine the photostability of the samples under UV light, as shown in Fig. 6 . The color of the CDs powder underwent a transformation, shifting from a grayish green to a black hue, accompanied by a notable reduction in size due to its high hygroscopicity. As expected, the blue emission intensity of the CDs powder exhibited a rapid decline over the course of 5 h, ultimately reaching a point of complete disappearance. This indicates that the CDs powder underwent a significant deterioration process, resulting in photobleaching. On the contrary, the NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs remained their white color and showed a clear red emission under UV light irradiation. This phenomenon is consistent with the previously reported absence of photobleaching and photodamage for Ln3+ ions doped nanocrystals [45]. In the case of the dry-mixed nanocomposite, the color gradually darkens and the blue emission weakens over time. This indicates that direct physical mixing may enhance the photostability of CDs powder to some extent. However, the intrinsic poor photostability of CDs persists due to the lack of robust interactions between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs in the physical mixture. The photostability of the CDs in the wet-mixed and the reacted nanocomposites is enhanced by the formation of strong interactions between the CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs. This is evidenced by the absence of photobleaching or photodamage observed after 5 h of continuous light illumination (see Fig. 6). This evidence serves to reinforce the robustness of sensitization via interfacial FRET from CDs to NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs.

In addition to photostability, thermal cycling stability is an important parameter for potential applications such as white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs). Fig. 7 a shows the temperature-dependent emission spectra for the CDs powder under excitation at 340 nm. With temperature increasing, the intensity of the broadband blue emission decreases dramatically, reaching a complete absence at 150 °C, suggesting poor thermal stability. More seriously, the CDs powder has been damaged at high temperatures, rendering it unable to emit photons (see inset of Fig. 7a). In contrast, the whole emission intensity of the reacted nanocomposite gradually decreases with increasing temperature, but still maintains 53 % of its initial value at 150 °C (see Fig. 7b). The reacted nanocomposite demonstrates superior thermal stability in comparison to the CDs powder. This indicates that the interactions between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs are robust, thereby boosting the thermal stability of the resulting nanocomposite. It should be noted that the decrease in Eu3+ emission at 700 nm is more gradual than that of CDs’ broadband emission at 450 nm. This can be proved by the ratio of I450/I700 as a function of temperature, as shown in Fig. 7c, which shows a gradual increase with rising temperature. This can be explained by the differing thermal quenching mechanisms of CDs and Eu3+ ions emissions. When the temperature is increased, non-radiative relaxations become thermally activated, such as the trapping of electrons by surface/defect/ionized impurity states, resulting in a loss of emission intensity [46]. Due to the specific ladder-like 4f energy level structures, the thermal quenching behavior of Eu3+ emission is mainly determined by the multi-phonon relaxation, which is less affected by the temperature. This indicates that the reacted nanocomposite has the potential to be used as a ratiometric luminescent thermometer for temperature sensing [47]. Fig. 7d shows the variation in emission intensity over four cycles, measured from 30 to 150 °C. When the reacted nanocomposite cooling back to 30 °C, the emission intensity recovers to its initial value, and more remarkably, this reversible emission response persists after several rounds of heating and cooling experiments. It is noteworthy that the emission profile and peak position exhibit minimal change with increasing temperature. The temperature robustness of the reacted nanocomposite confers a significant advantage upon it in comparison to other CDs-based nanocomposites, which are suitable for use in photoelectric devices and biomedical applications.

In summary, we conducted a comprehensive investigation into the interfacial FRET between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs in nanocomposites prepared by various methods. The interfacial interaction between CDs and NaYF4:Eu3+ NPs is sufficiently robust to permit interfacial FRET from the CDs to Eu3+ in both the wet-mixed and reacted nanocomposites. The presence of interfacial FRET is further demonstrated by the broadened excitation spectra and the shortened luminescence decay curves. Interestingly, the reacted nanocomposite presents a superior photostability and thermal cycling stability compared to the physical mixture. The nanocomposites not only realize the solid-state luminescence of CDs, but also provide a feasible way to construct efficient interfacial FRET systems. This study presents a new strategy to engineer CDs-based nanocomposites, which can be better meet the increasing demand for multifunctional materials in a range of applications.
There are no conflicts to declare.

Xiaoyi Wu: Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yeqing Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Rui Li: Investigation, Data curation. Qingguang Zeng: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ting Yu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
